text: The algorithms think I’m a movie director, a journalist said that I design wonderful and terrifying future things, my grandma still wants to believe that I’m an engineer, when I say I’m a designer, most people ask me of what…
I normally say that I design future products, sometimes real and sometimes fictional.*
footnote: Yeah I know, it’s a bit of a cop out, but it worked so far.

text: Right now I’m exploring how to create products for a less boring future as a co-founder and futures director at oio.

I have designed future worlds, products and experiences for companies and organisations around the world. I was part in developing the first museum of the future in the world, worked on AI-assisted design and future mobility for BMW, the first smart pollution mask for Xiaomi, very very secret stuff with Google X and some non-human intelligence inspired assistants for Google AI.*
footnote: I have also really worked for them… not just saying it. And for other companies like frog, Philips and Logitech. At some point I was designing a lot of masks… before corona times btw.

text: I really like simple and mundane things and use them to imagine parallel presents and near futures.
I made masks that can smile for you, toasters that can sell themselves, and plugs that have political systems within them.

Generally I think I’m a bit of an animist, but after IoT and AI who isn’t these days… I love Roombas as I think they are the most underestimated non-human intelligence that actually made it in our homes.*

I think I’m not that good at really designing stuff so I explore ways in which machines can help me do that. I made a fictional company that generates Chinese/Scandinavian mashup products, generated a series of plates emerging from abundance of materials, spoons co-designed with algorithms and craftsmen, and chairs that evolve.

Sometimes when I don’t want to make an actual product I explore futures through different mediums. I wrote fables about more-than-human futures, made immersive installations about being an appliance with Bruce Sterling, and also made a short movie that won the Most Uncanny award at the Robot Film Festival.
I like to speak about new ways to design with AI, more-than-human futures, the difference between real and fictional—and I used to speak a lot about toasters.

I am in the faculty for the first Master on Responsible AI at Elisava in Barcelona, visiting lecturer at Copenhagen Institute of Interaction Design and other universities around Europe. Some workshops are about becoming objects, some are about imagining a future of trainable interfaces or when AI and robots are truly domesticated.
Some journalists said my work is “wonderful and terrifying all at once”—I’ll take it.

At oio we call ourselves a hybrid team of humans and machines, which sounds more mysterious than it is—we’re just designers who got very interested in what it means to build products when your collaborator doesn’t sleep, doesn’t get bored, and occasionally hallucinates.

I used to think designers were the good guys. Then I spent enough time actually building things and realised we’re also part of the problem. That’s not a reason to stop, but it is a reason to design differently.

At Milan Design Week we made The Last Pencil—an exploration of what drawing tools become when intelligence gets baked into everything. The pencil is maybe the most honest design tool ever made. We wanted to find out what happens to honesty when you add a model.

With IKEA’s research lab we made Updatables—furniture that evolves with evolutionary algorithms. A chair that knows who sits in it, a shelf that reorganises around you. It sounds convenient until you realise your home might have opinions about how you live.

With oio and Italian silversmith Greggio Argenterie we made Spawns—a collection of silver spoons where a generative algorithm analysed thousands of historical tableware images to suggest new forms, which were then hand-forged by craftsmen. Artisanal intelligence, we called it.
I wrote a book of fables called Everything is Someone—stories about futures where objects, animals, and systems have something like interiority. It felt more honest than a white paper.*
footnote: It’s a real book. You can buy it. Vetro Editions.

text: We also run oio.school, where people come to work with us on future products. It’s part studio, part experiment in what design education looks like when the tools change faster than the curriculum.
I spent five years in China, which changed how I think about scale, speed, and the gap between how things are imagined and how they end up being made. Also about hotpot.
I live in Belgrade now, which is a city that has seen enough futures arrive and disappear to be appropriately sceptical about all of them. It suits me.
